nativeRange.habitat: Dense vegetation along margins of rivers and floodplain lagoons; found in lagoons and backwaters in shallow water just beneath surface vegetation; slow-moving to standing water over soft substrate
nativeRange.biotope: African river/lagoon — soft, heavily vegetated margins of the Okavango, upper Zambezi, Kafue, and Cunene river systems; also northern Lake Malawi shoreline

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (essential for natural foraging and resting)
dense planting with broad-leaved plants (Java fern, Anubias, Amazon sword)
floating plants to diffuse light and reduce surface brightness
driftwood caves, PVC tubes, or coconut hides for daytime refuge
dim or subdued lighting to encourage daytime activity
minimal water current — prefers still to gently filtered water

diet.notes: Feeds on aquatic insect larvae in the wild, primarily Chironomidae (bloodworm midges). In captivity, accepts frozen bloodworms, blackworms, and white/micro worms. Live foods (live bloodworms, live black worms, daphnia, mosquito larvae) are strongly preferred and important for conditioning shy specimens. Typically refuses dry pellets and flakes — prepared foods are rarely accepted, especially in newly acquired fish. Feed near the substrate at dusk or after lights-out using a feeding tube or turkey baster to target the fish directly. Once settled, freeze-dried bloodworms may occasionally be accepted.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetras, cardinal tetras)
dwarf corydoras (kept off competition for bottom space)
other small non-aggressive bottom dwellers that do not compete for food
avoidWith: other mormyrids and Pollimyrus conspecifics (territorial; electric interference)
aggressive or boisterous fish that will out-compete at feeding
fast surface feeders that consume food before it reaches the bottom
any fish requiring salt or brackish conditions

imageCandidates.notes: Verify this image path corresponds to Pollimyrus castelnaui on FishBase summary page; confirm before use.

summary: The Baby Whale is a small (~7–9 cm) weakly electric African mormyrid from the Okavango and Zambezi river systems. It uses its caudal electric organ for electrolocation and communication rather than offense. Strictly nocturnal, demanding to feed (frozen/live insect larvae only), and territorial toward conspecifics; best kept singly in a quiet, dimly lit specialist setup.
careNotes:
The Baby Whale (Pollimyrus castelnaui) is a member of the family Mormyridae, the weakly electric elephantfishes of Africa. Like all mormyrids, it generates low-voltage electric organ discharges (EODs) from a caudal-peduncle organ for electrolocation in murky water and for intraspecific communication and mate recognition — not for stunning prey or defense. The species is native to the Okavango basin, upper Zambezi, Kafue, and Cunene river systems, as well as the northern Lake Malawi shoreline, inhabiting slow backwaters and lagoons with dense marginal vegetation.

All specimens in the aquarium trade are wild-caught; captive breeding has not been achieved. This, combined with feeding specialisation, makes the Baby Whale a fish for experienced, committed keepers. A minimum 200 L tank with a long footprint (100 cm+) is recommended; the species is strictly bottom-oriented and rarely ventures to mid or upper water. Fine sand at least 3 cm deep is essential. Lighting should be subdued — floating plants or a dimmer setup will greatly improve daytime visibility of the fish and reduce stress. Dense planting and multiple cave-type hides (driftwood, PVC tubes, coconut shells) are required.

Feeding is the principal challenge: freshly imported specimens almost universally refuse prepared foods. Begin with live bloodworms or live blackworms delivered near the substrate with a feeding tube, ideally 30–60 minutes after the room lights go out. Frozen bloodworms and frozen white worms can be introduced once the fish is feeding confidently. Routine refusal of flakes and pellets should be expected — do not rely on prepared foods for this species. A single specimen kept in a quiet tank with no food competition will usually begin feeding within 1–3 weeks.

The Baby Whale is territorial toward conspecifics and other mormyrids; EODs from competing individuals cause stress. Keep singly or in a sufficiently large tank where individuals can establish separate territories (not reliably achievable in typical home aquaria). Suitable tankmates are small, peaceful, free-swimming species that do not compete for bottom space or food. Salt — even at trace aquarium-salt doses — is to be avoided, as dissolved ions at unnatural concentrations disrupt the electroreceptor system. Copper-based medications are contraindicated; scaleless or thin-scaled skin renders these fish highly copper-sensitive. Use copper-free treatments (formalin, methylene blue, salt-free alternatives) for disease management.

Water quality must be excellent: the species is sensitive to ammonia and nitrite accumulation. Weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are recommended. Aim for soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.5–7.2, gH 5–15) matching the softwater rivers of its native range. Avoid hard or alkaline tap water without conditioning.

See also: [elephant-nose] (Gnathonemus petersii) — the classic aquarium mormyrid, larger (20–25 cm) with a proboscis; a distinct species often confused in the trade with the Baby Whale.
breedingNotes: Not bred in captivity. All aquarium specimens are wild-caught. Sexual dimorphism has not been described for aquarium purposes, though research populations show differences in electric organ discharge (EOD) waveform between sexes. No reliable breeding protocol exists for hobbyists.

primary.name: FishBase — Pollimyrus castelnaui
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (max 7.0 cm SL), native range (Okavango basin, upper Zambezi, Kafue, Cunene rivers; northern Lake Malawi; Luapula/Congo in Zambia; Kasai in Angola), habitat (dense marginal vegetation, floodplain lagoons, shallow backwaters), diet (aquatic insect larvae, primarily Chironomidae), trophic level 3.2, demersal freshwater habit, IUCN Least Concern. Water parameters not provided by FishBase; drawn from hobbyist and wiki sources below.

additional.name: The Aquarium Wiki — Pollimyrus castelnaui (Dwarf Stonebasher)
additional.notes: Provided water parameters (temperature 23.9–25.6°C, pH 6.5–7.5, hardness 6–16 dH), minimum tank size 170 L, behavior notes (territorial toward conspecifics, bottom dweller, prefers dense cover), and care difficulty (moderate/difficult due to wild-caught status). Temperature range broadened slightly based on broader mormyrid care literature.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Dwarf Stonebasher
additional.notes: Confirmed weakly electric status, southern African distribution (Angola, Namibia, Botswana), IUCN Least Concern, difficult maintenance due to all specimens being wild-caught, aquarium trade presence. Notes average length as 2 cm (likely SL of juveniles or a different measurement convention); FishBase max 7.0 cm SL used as authoritative adult size.

additional.name: AZGardens — Mormyrid Baby Whale (Marcusenius brachyistius)
additional.notes: Trade care sheet for a closely related mormyrid sold as ‘baby whale’. Provided temperature 72–82°F (22–28°C), pH 6.3–7.4, hardness 2–18 dH, fine sand or gravel substrate, daytime hiding requirements, nocturnal behavior, live/frozen food preference, weak electric organ description. Data used to cross-check and supplement Pollimyrus-specific parameters.

additional.name: Springer — Evidence for Parapatric Speciation in Pollimyrus castelnaui (Environmental Biology of Fishes)
additional.notes: Peer-reviewed research confirming Okavango–Upper Zambezi distribution, EOD waveform differentiation between sexes and sister species (P. marianne), and electric organ location in caudal peduncle. Supports EOD-based communication and species recognition notes in careNotes.

fish.breedingNotes: Not bred in captivity; all trade specimens are wild-caught. No hobbyist breeding protocol exists. Sex differences expressed in EOD waveform rather than obvious external morphology.
